content:
Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes Elena Medri a, Freya Bretton b

, Maria-Chiara Ferrari b , Chunchun Ye c

, Neil B. McKeown c, Marco Giacinti Baschetti a,* a Dipartimento di Ingegneria Civile, Chimica, Ambientale e dei Materiali (DICAM), Alma Mater StudiorumUniversit`a di Bologna, via Terracini 28, 40131, Bologna, Italyb Institute for Materials and Processes, School of Engineering, The University of Edinburgh, Mayfield Road, Edinburgh, EH9 3JL, UKc EaStCHEM, School of Chemistry, University of Edinburgh, David Brewster Road, Edinburgh, EH9 3FJ, UK

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:PIM-EA-TBPIM-SBFAO-PIM-1Water sorptionCO2 captureHumid gas permeation

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: A B S T R A C T The present study investigates the impact of relative humidity on the permeability and selectivity of three polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs), namely PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF, and AO-PIM-1. To that aim, single gas permeation tests were conducted at 25 ◦C and 1 bar with nitrogen and carbon dioxide with different humidity levels, from 0 to 80 %. Water sorption was measured independently to correlate relative humidity to the water content in the membrane. The effect of water vapor on polymer ageing was also considered, by testing the same sample multiple times. Results indicate that water vapor strongly affects the permeability of the three materials; a drastic decrease in CO2 permeability, higher than 90 %, was observed for all the polymers when increasing the relative humidity of the gas. In addition to that, in the case of PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF, permeability was not completely recovered after the 30 days of testing due to irreversible changes likely caused by ageing and water exposure. After three permeation runs in humid conditions the permeability of dry CO2 decreased, from 2285 to 249 Barrer and from 4070 to 1150 Barrer for PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF respectively. In AO-PIM-1, on the other hand, dry gas permeability remained substantially unchanged even after exposure to water vapor.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: TB and PIM-SBF respectively. In AO-PIM-1, on the other hand, dry gas permeability remained substantially unchanged even after exposure to water vapor. This difference is also observed in sorption experiments which showed a strong decrease in water uptake in subsequent tests for PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF while smaller reductions were observed for AO-PIM-1. CO2/N2 selectivity was in the order of 10 and 25 for the PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF and remained substantially unchanged during the tests, while for AO-PIM-1 a more than two-fold increase was observed, from about 40 to more than 100, when relative humidity was increased from 0 to 80 %. This increase, coupled with a CO2 permeability still higher than 200 Barrer, allowed this polymer to surpass the 2019 Robeson’s plot for the CO2/N2 gas pair.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: 1. Introduction The need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions has prompted exten­sive research into innovative carbon dioxide capture strategies, partic­ularly focusing on emissions from large industrial sources [1,2]. Among these, post-combustion membrane separation emerges as a promising avenue, offering several advantages thanks to its low energy intensity, high operative flexibility, and small dimensions [3,4]. The unique con­ditions of post-combustion flue gases, characterized by low pressure, however, necessitate the development of membranes exhibiting better properties in terms of selectivity and permeability, compared to those currently available on the market [5]. Challenges in utilizing membranes for CO2 capture arise from the need to achieve the required separation targets, that is 95 % purity and 90 % recovery, as stipulated by the U.S. Department of Energy [6].

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content:
Currently, commercially available gas separation membranes rely on a limited set of polymers characterized by relatively low permeability and high selectivity. Consequently, a substantial membrane area is necessary to compensate for the low flux at the low driving force for permeation typical of post combustion, escalating the cost and spatial requirements for implementation in industrial applications. Hence, there is a pressing demand for membranes that exhibit higher permeability while main­taining satisfactory selectivity for effective post-combustion CO2 capture [7]. In the realm of polymeric materials, Polymers of Intrinsic Micropo­rosity (PIMs) have emerged as promising candidates for various appli­cations due to their unique structure. PIMs indeed possess a continuous network of interconnected molecular voids (around 2 nm) resulting from the unique shape and rigidity of their macromolecules [8–11]. The interconnected free volume elements within the PIMs structure result in

* Corresponding author.E-mail address: marco.giacinti@unibo.it (M. Giacinti Baschetti).

Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Journal of Membrane Science journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/memsci

Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: Available online 17 May 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync-nd/4.0/ ). significantly high permeability for gas molecules without compromising the selectivity of the material. Despite the high free volume that limits the molecular sieving ability of most PIM membranes, indeed, good selectivity for carbon dioxide is often observed, which is primarily attributed to the high condensability of the carbon dioxide molecule compared to other gases [10–12]. Commonly, PIMs are formed by two main components: a structural unit with concavities that introduce a contortion site on the polymer chain, and a linking group that fuses the structural units during poly­merization but prohibits or strongly hinders their relative rotation. The first reported microporous polymer was PIM-1 [13], with spirobisindane (SBI) as a structural unit and a fused linear unit based on benzodioxin as a linking group, which was then followed by many modifications often related to the addition to the main chain of different side groups [14]. Literature reports promising results for these materials given their application as post combustion membranes; for example, CO2 perme­ability of up to 7010 Barrer was observed for PIM-1 coupled with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 19.5 [15], while values close to 1100 Barrer with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 26.7 were reported for PIM-7 [16].

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: IM-1 coupled with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 19.5 [15], while values close to 1100 Barrer with a CO2/N2 selectivity of 26.7 were reported for PIM-7 [16]. These data highlight their potential for overcoming the traditional trade-off be­tween permeability and selectivity in membranes and have been used in the most recent revision of Robeson’s Upper bound made in 2019 [17] Amidoxime-functionalized polymer of intrinsic microporosity AO-PIM-1 is another example in which amidoxime (AO) groups decorated the pristine PIM-1 backbone, [18,19]; it presents a CO2 permeability of 1153 Barrer and a CO2/N2 selectivity of 35 [20]. Building upon the success of SBI-based PIMs, researchers are increasingly utilizing different monomers to produce PIMs that are even more rigid, due to the use of different structural units such as spirobi­fluorene (SBF), where the fused benzo groups enhance rigidity about the spiro-carbon, or ethanoanthracene (EA), triptycene (Trip) and Tr¨oger’s base (TB) endowed with highly rigid bridged bicyclic elements. Among them, PIM-EA-TB, which displays exceptional molecular sieving prop­erties related to high intrachain rigidity of the bridged bicyclic TB and EA, was studied by several authors who reported CO2 permeability around 5100 Barrer and CO2/N2 selectivity of 13.4 [21–23].

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: he bridged bicyclic TB and EA, was studied by several authors who reported CO2 permeability around 5100 Barrer and CO2/N2 selectivity of 13.4 [21–23]. Even higher CO2 permeability values were obtained by using PIM-SBF (8850 Barrer) or PIM-Trip-TB (9709 Barrer) which also presented interesting CO2/N2 selectivity of 26 and 15.4, respectively [24,25]. For the sake of completeness, it should be said that most of the previous data refers to fresh samples and does not account for the decrease in permeability related to ageing. As most high free volume glassy polymers, indeed, also PIMs tend to decrease their permeability with time, due to the collapse of free volume elements. Ageing phe­nomena were reported in many PIMs: PIM-Trip-TB showed decreased its CO2 permeability from 9709 to 3751 Barrer in 100 days while PIM-EATB thick films, showed a reduction from 7140 to 2644 Barrer in 470 days [26]. PIM-Btrip-TB showed a decrease from 13200 Barrer in CO2 permeability down to 4150 Barrer in 166 days [27]. Interestingly, due to the reduction in the free volume, and thus, a decrease in the diffusion coefficient, ageing in most cases leads to an increase in the membrane selectivity so that final membrane properties are still of interest and sometimes even better than the fresh polymer, at least in the medium term.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: ane selectivity so that final membrane properties are still of interest and sometimes even better than the fresh polymer, at least in the medium term. Nonetheless, many attempts have been made to reduce the ten­dency of PIM to age by further increasing the chain rigidity through suitable modification of the polymer chain chemistry [28] or by adding fillers [29]. In addition to ageing issues, PIM polymers are often tested in ideal conditions, so that the permeability and selectivity values typically re­ported in the literature do not reflect real post-combustion conditions. One crucial difference is the presence of impurities such as SOx, NOx, as well as water vapor which are always present in the effluent stream. Water vapor, among the others, is known to have a significant effect on the transport properties of polymers both in hydrophilic and hydro­phobic materials. In particular, in hydrophobic polymers, water vapor tends to occupy the free volume of the polymers due to its high condensability, making the space unavailable for other diffusing gases [30–32]. In hydrophilic membranes, on the other hand, it is expected to swell the polymer, leading to an increase in free volume and perme­ability [33–35]. Therefore, it is vital to understand the full effects of water vapor on PIMs and their gas permeation parameters to determine their usefulness for post-combustion carbon capture.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: derstand the full effects of water vapor on PIMs and their gas permeation parameters to determine their usefulness for post-combustion carbon capture. To the best of our knowledge, however, the water impact on gas permeability has been studied only on PIM-1, revealing a significant decrease in CO2 and N2 permeability as humidity levels rise [15]. The aim of the present study is to expand the scope of investigations beyond PIM-1, considering specifically PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF and AO-PIM-1, which are showing potential for CCS applications. In particular, a se­ries of permeation tests have been performed with CO2 and N2 at different relative humidity investigating the effect of water vapor on permeability. In addition, water vapor sorption tests were carried out as well to correlate the relative humidity to the actual water content in the membrane. The goal of this study is to provide a comprehensive understanding of the correlation between humidity, polymer structure, and gas transport properties contributing to the ongoing research for highly efficient postcombustion carbon dioxide capture technologies. 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials The polymers studied in this work, namely PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF, and AO-PIM-1 (Fig. 1), were synthesized in the School of Chemistry at the University of Edinburgh. The synthetic routes are all reported in the open literature [23,25,36] and will not be described here.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: l of Chemistry at the University of Edinburgh. The synthetic routes are all reported in the open literature [23,25,36] and will not be described here. The mem­branes were produced by film casting from chloroform solution for PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF while dimethylformamide solution was used for AO-PIM-1, followed by slow evaporation. The thickness of the ob­tained films was in the order of 100–180 μm, as measured with a flat plate micrometer (Mitutoyo Absolute Series 227-221). Permeation tests were performed on a sample masked with aluminum foil to reduce the permeation area to about 2.2 cm2. All the films were soaked in methanol for 24 h to erase previous history and were then dried in air 24 h before the masking procedure. The masking requested about 8 h and was followed by a final pretreatment, 24 h in a dynamic vacuum at 25 ◦C, made on the sample already inside the permeation apparatus to ensure removal of all residual solvents [37]. This was also confirmed by the absence of detectable methanol char­acteristic peak in the IR spectra of the treated samples. Chloroform, methanol, and dimethylformamide were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further modifications. Permeation tests are performed using pure N2 and CO2 (purity of 99.99 %), provided by Fluido Tecnica s.a.s (Campi Bisenzio, Firenze, Italy). 2.2. FTIR An Avatar 360 E S P.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: performed using pure N2 and CO2 (purity of 99.99 %), provided by Fluido Tecnica s.a.s (Campi Bisenzio, Firenze, Italy). 2.2. FTIR An Avatar 360 E S P. spectrometer was used in the Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) configuration using a Pike MIRacle Single Reflection ATR system endowed with a ZnSe crystal to verify possible film modi­fication upon contact with water. Spectra of the different materials were acquired in a spectral range of 400–4000 cm-1 with a 4 cm-1 resolution; 16 scans were averaged to obtain each spectrum to increase the signalto-noise ratio. The different materials were tested before and after the experimental campaign to investigate possible modifications due to the interaction between the humid gases and the polymeric matrix. 2.3. Permeation measurements The gas permeability measurements were performed using the fixed volume-variable pressure thermostatic system shown in Fig. 2 specif­ically built to perform mixed gas permeation tests in a closed system

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: [38]. An agitator is inserted in the 2L upstream gas reservoir, to ensure total homogenization of the gas phase during mixed-gas permeation experiments avoiding issues related to the concentration polarization phenomenon. The change in concentration is considered negligible due to the large upstream volume, about 2L, compared to the downstream one which is around 30 cm3. The tests were conducted at a temperature of 25 ◦C controlled by a thermostatic bath (Julabo-Corio C, reading error ±0.1 ◦C). The upstream pressure was measured via a Druck manometer (range 0.02–10 bar, reading error 0.05 % of the full scale), and the downstream pressure by a capacitive manometer (1000 mbar full scale, reading error 0.25 % of the reading). Before the permeation tests, the membranes were pretreated as previously described in the Material section to lower the variability due to previous history which is known to affect the properties of glassy polymers. After that, a series of dry and humid tests were performed. Each film was assessed under different humidity namely 0 %, 20 %, 40 %, 60 %, and 80 % in the first tests while in additional repetition lower number of relative humidity values were considered to speed up the completion of the experimental campaign.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: tests while in additional repetition lower number of relative humidity values were considered to speed up the completion of the experimental campaign. Dry permeation tests were conducted by degassing both sides of the membrane in a dynamic vacuum at 25 ◦C for at least 1 h to then wait for pressure stabilization (leak check) before the gas was fed to the up­stream compartment at the desired pressure (1 bar in the present campaign) to start the permeation process. The permeability, P, was calculated from the increase of pressure, p, in the calibrated downstream volume, Vd, through Eq. (1) below. P =(dpd dt ) s.s. ⋅VdRTA.δ(pu,i -pd,i )(1) Where ( dpd dt ) s.s. is the steady state pressure change in time in the downstream compartment, A the membrane area, δ is the thickness of the membrane (considered constant during the experiments), (pu,i -pd,i) is the partial pressure difference of component i across the membrane, T the test temperature, and R the ideal gas constant. After the test, a dy­namic vacuum (for at least one 1h) was again applied to the cell and then the sample was maintained in a static vacuum overnight, before changing the operative conditions and starting a new measurement. For the humid tests, the procedure was the same as previously described, but each membrane was equilibrated at the desired relative humidity (RH) before the beginning of the test.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: edure was the same as previously described, but each membrane was equilibrated at the desired relative humidity (RH) before the beginning of the test. This was made by filling both the upstream and downstream compartments with a suitable pressure of water vapor, pw, until equilibrium was reached. After this procedure, which usually took about 2h, the test proceeded for dry gases and Eq. (1) was used to retrieve permeability; indeed, as the humidity was balanced on both sides of the membrane before testing, no driving force was present for permeation of the water vapor across the mem­brane - ensuring no water flow occurs during the tests. The precision of the system, considering the noise of the measurement and the other uncertainties can be considered in the order of ±5 % for dry gas test and slightly higher for humid tests due to additional uncertainty in the control of RH. For each sample, at least three experimental runs were performed with each gas investigated to elucidate also a possible effect of water vapor on the polymer ageing. 2.4. Water vapor sorption test Water sorption tests were performed at a temperature of 25 ◦C by using a Quartz Spring Microbalance (QSM) schematized in Fig. 3 and already described elsewhere [39].

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: tests were performed at a temperature of 25 ◦C by using a Quartz Spring Microbalance (QSM) schematized in Fig. 3 and already described elsewhere [39]. This equipment consists of a quartz spring with a known elastic constant (0.00215 g/mm), housed within a thermostatic glass column, the temperature of which is monitored by a temperature indicator (TI02).The pressure and the expansion volume’s temperature are checked by a pressure indicator (PI01) and a temper­ature indicator (TI01), respectively. The studied sample, of known weight and thickness, is attached to the bottom of the spring whose position is monitored and registered by a dedicated CCD camera. During the test, the mass uptake causes an elongation of the spring which is recorded by the CCD to give information about the sorption process. A

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 dynamic reference of a known dimension is used to convert the displacement from pixel to millimeter. The sample, after being soaked for 24h in methanol and dried in air for 24h, was securely attached to the spring and was subjected to a dynamic vacuum for 24 h to eliminate any pre-existing moisture content and methanol. Afterward, water uptake is measured by gradually increasing the RH from 10 % to about 90 %. At each RH increment, the elongation of the spring, induced by water adsorption, is recorded until equilibrium pressure is achieved. The water uptake at the ith step has been evaluated by: (mH20)i = (hi,t→∞-h0 )kg(2) Where k is for spring constant (g/mm), hi,t→∞ represents the spring length at equilibrium, h0 initial spring length and g is the gravity ac­celeration; the buoyancy force was neglected during the tests due to limited pressure involved. The precision of the system, considering the noise of the measurement and the other uncertainties can be considered in the order of ±0.1 mg. Considering the mass (in the order of 300 mg) of the sampled used for tests, average relative errors in the order of 5–8 % were observed for the different polymers. Data at low relative hu­midity were characterized by the higher uncertainty which in any case never exceeded 20 %.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: bserved for the different polymers. Data at low relative hu­midity were characterized by the higher uncertainty which in any case never exceeded 20 %. In addition to the equilibrium value of water uptake the sorption test also allowed the evaluation of the water diffusion coefficient, D, in the matrix, which could be estimated by fitting the mass change as a func­tion of time with a suitable solution of the Fick’s Law, that for the case under consideration can be written as: mH20(t)mH20eq = 1 -8π2 ∑ n (2n + 1)2 exp[-(2n + 1)2(π δ )2 Dt](3)

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content:
Where, mH20eq is the equilibrium mass uptake at the end of the single sorption test and δ represents the thickness of the sample used. As for the permeation, also sorption tests were repeated at least three times on the same sample, to investigate possible modification of the sorption isotherm likely due to film ageing.

3. Results and discussion 3.1. Water sorption Fig. 4 shows the sorption isotherms obtained in the first experimental run of PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF, and AO-PIM-1. The water uptake (g/gpol­ ymer) of the films is reported as a function of water activity, calculated as the ratio between pressure and vapor pressure at the experimental temperature. The results clearly indicates that the affinity for water vapor increases going from PIM-SBF, which exhibits the lowest water uptake, never exceeding 0.03 g/gp, to AO-PIM-1, which remains in the middle despite the presence of polar and hydrophilic side groups, to PIM-EA-TB. Indeed, the latter polymer, likely due to the presence of Tr¨oger’s base groups which may present hydrophilic behavior [40] as well as the higher surface area and larger pores, exhibits the higher water uptake reaching 0.15 g/gpol at about 90 % relative humidity in the first run; the data aligns with those reported by Vopiˇcka et al. [41] that found similar uptake and the same qualitative behavior of the water sorption isotherm in PIM-EA-TB observed in this work. As said above, even if endowed with hydrophilic groups, AO-PIM-1

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 shows a maximum water uptake not exceeding 0.10 g/gpol and remains lower than what was measured for PIM-EA-TB. Nonetheless, the different chemical structure of the two materials can be correlated to the different trends observed at lower water activities (up to 40 %). In this range, the PIM-EA-TB shows a negligible increase in water uptake, suggesting difficulty in water penetration into the available free volume. Conversely, AO-PIM-1 shows higher values of solubility at low humidity, more than double compared to those of PIM-EA-TB, reflecting the hy­drophilic nature of the material. It seems, indeed, that the voids of AOPIM-1 polymer are more ready to receive water molecules than those of PIM-EA-TB, which somewhat activate only a high activity following a trend usually observed in porous materials characterized by multilayer adsorption process. As said above, each film has been tested in at least three successive tests, for a total duration of the experiments of about 10 days. This procedure was implemented to study the time evolution of water sorp­tion for each polymer, which, once again, can be subjected to changes related to polymer ageing. The results are reported in Fig. 5a and b and c, where the comparison between successive sorption curves are depicted for PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF, and AO-PIM-1, respectively. The figures reveal a common behavior. For instance, Fig.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: n successive sorption curves are depicted for PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF, and AO-PIM-1, respectively. The figures reveal a common behavior. For instance, Fig. 5a shows an evident reduction of the sorption by PIM-EA-TB, especially at the higher activities inspected, set equal to 80 % RH, where the uptake is lowered from 0.15 g/gpol observed in the first test down to 0.02 g/gpol for the last test with a reduction of about 1 order of magnitude. Similar trends are observed for PIM-SBF and AO-PIM-1, though with varying degrees of reduction. In PIM-SBF, solubility decreases by about 6 times from a value of 0.024 g/gpol in the first run to 0.004 g/gpol in the last run, while AO-PIM-1, exhibits a more moderate decline, of about a half, with up­take decreasing from 0.09 g/g(pol) for the first run to 0.05 g/g(pol) in the last test. Similar decreasing behavior of solubility in PIMs was sometimes related to incomplete desorption, which in some cases has also been reported for water. Yampolskii et al. reported that water could only be removed completely from AO-PIM-1 by heating the polymer at 160 ◦C. In PIM-1 [42], on the other hand, it has been observed that the sorption of water onto the membrane surface results in a reduction in the solu­bility coefficient, which can only be restored by multiple days of drying the membrane.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: r onto the membrane surface results in a reduction in the solu­bility coefficient, which can only be restored by multiple days of drying the membrane. In the present case, an attentive analysis of possible water residue after sorption was not carried out; however, even if some residual water is visible in the IR spectra of the polymer as will be dis­cussed below, the final weight of the membrane was restored after the vacuum treatment made at the end of the test; differences, were indeed generally negligible, in the same order of spring precision, and in any case definitely smaller than the reductions subsequently observed in the value of water vapor uptake. Notably, the polymer exhibiting the smallest decrease in water up­take, AO-PIM-1, contains hydrophilic side groups which may help maintain high water affinity even as ageing reduces the free volume available for sorption. It can be said that this PIM may possibly present some swelling despite the rigidity of the polymer backbone [43]. This is not the case for the other polymers, where solubility seems to be driven by the ability of water to enter and occupy the voids already available in the polymer-free volume. This is especially evident in PIM-SBF, where the second and third sorption isotherms present a more and more pro­nounced plateau with respect to the first sorption test. This suggests that, as the polymer ages, it becomes less able to accommodate water mole­cules outside the most favorable free-volume elements.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: n test. This suggests that, as the polymer ages, it becomes less able to accommodate water mole­cules outside the most favorable free-volume elements. A similar conclusion may be drawn for PIM-EA-TB, which also experiences a decrease in solubility, which is particularly pronounced at higher water activity, where a pore filling or multilayer adsorption mechanism should be considered to explain the observed upturn of the solubility isotherm. This hypothesis is supported by the fact that, considering the free vol­ume fraction of PIM-EA-TB, estimated in the order of 0.27 from MD simulations [44], and the observed water uptake, only 15 % of the free volume is occupied by water, making swelling an unlike event. The decrease of such uptake in subsequent sorption runs could therefore be related to ageing triggered by a decrease of fractional free volume and of pore dimension in the matrix. A more detailed analysis of the water behavior inside the investi­gated PIMs is outside the scope of the present work; however, for the sake of completeness it should be noticed that, the effect of aging on water uptake in PIMs material seems to be somewhat peculiar with Fig. 5. Water Sorption Isotherm(T = 25 ◦C) for subsequential runs. a) PIM-EATB; b) PIM-SBF; c) AO-PIM-1.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 respect to what currently available in the open literature. Indeed, the very few studies that analyzed ageing effect on sorption are focused on non-condensable gases such as carbon dioxide, and show that ageing primarily affects diffusivity, while the solubility coefficient remains almost constant. [45,46]. In this concern, the effect observed in the present work seems to be related to the high condensability and polarity of water vapor. As an example, structural rearrangements or densifica­tion within the polymer matrix that limit cluster formation may disproportionately affect polar and condensable species with respect to non-condensable gases. More comprehensive studies, however, are needed in order to verify such an assumption. To obtain a more complete picture of water transport in the polymer, water diffusivity calculated through Eq. (3) for the three different polymers is reported in Fig. 6, as a function of water activity. Notably, the trend of diffusivity differs significantly for the three polymers tested confirming their different behavior in the presence of water. In PIM-SBF, the diffusion coefficient is substantially constant in the activity range inspected; it presents only a slight decrease from 1.510-8 to 110-8 cm2/s with a change which is indeed very close to instrument sensitivity. This behavior is in line with the very low solubility shown by these materials.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: /s with a change which is indeed very close to instrument sensitivity. This behavior is in line with the very low solubility shown by these materials. Water content is indeed too low to change the polymer structure or to fill the free volume of the matrix enough to substantially affect the diffusion process. Concerning PIM-EA-TB, the diffusion coefficient at low humidity is higher (about one order of magnitude) than those observed in PIM-SBF, which might be related to a more favorable environment that facilitates the water jumps among different free volume elements. However, it also shows a decreasing behavior with water activity, suggesting that, as the amount of water in the membrane increases, the polymer experiences a significant reduction in the free volume available for diffusion or the initiation of water clustering inside the matrix voids. In PIM-1, as an example, it has been shown that residual water at concentration as low as few percent caused a decrease of the dimension of free volume ele­ments as measured through PALS analysis [42]. Decreasing behavior is finally shown also by AO-PIM-1, which pre­sent a diffusion coefficient in the order of 110-7 cm2/s at low humidity which is then reduced at high water content. However, the final value of the diffusion coefficient, at RH close to 85 %, remains in the order of 710-8 cm2/s, which is higher than what was observed for the other PIMs.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: lue of the diffusion coefficient, at RH close to 85 %, remains in the order of 710-8 cm2/s, which is higher than what was observed for the other PIMs. The presence of hydrophilic groups in the polymer therefore seems to facilitate water diffusion even at high concentrations reducing the effect of free volume filling or clustering which seems to hinder the transport in other PIMs tested.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: 3.2. Pure gas humid permeability The experimental campaign on permeability was focused on the analysis of the permeation of CO2 and N2 in the different materials in the presence of water vapor. Tests were repeated at least three times for each gas and each material to investigate also the impact on membrane ageing. Initial dry-state characterization of PIM-EA-TB, AO-PIM-1, and PIM-SBF is summarized in Table 1, where the experimental results are also compared with literature values. The observed dry values for both PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF fall below the ones reported in the literature, while AO-PIM-1 shows a higher result than the literature, despite the latter data being obtained at a higher temperature equal to 35 ◦C. The differences are rather high, but it is well known that this kind of comparison is for high free volume glassy polymer rather difficult, due to the strong effect of the past history on the materials properties. For example, differences as high as 200 % could be observed by changing the pretreatment in PIM-1 samples [42]. In the present case, a potential reason to explain the observed difference is related to the masking procedure, which increased by at least 8 h the time between the methanol treatment and the dry tests, which were made after additional 24h vacuum treatment was considered to degas the sample inside the permeation system. In this concern, “older” specimens compared to those tested in the literature were considered to start the present analysis.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: de the permeation system. In this concern, “older” specimens compared to those tested in the literature were considered to start the present analysis. As a reference it can be recalled that, in the case of PIM-EA-TB, reductions of up to 30 % in gas permeability were observed after only 4 h ageing under vacuum [21]. In addition, it is worthwhile to notice that some of the data reported in the table were obtained considering also a high temperature vacuum treatment which can indeed have induced additional differences in the polymer initial free volume distribution [21,49]. In addition, selectivity data reported in the literature are slightly lower than those obtained in the present study for all three polymers, as often observed when comparing data of new and aged samples. In any case, all three polymers still exhibit very high values of CO2 permeability and high CO2/N2 selectivity, so the current data can still be considered reliable and in line with general transport properties for the materials inspected. Following the initial dry-state test, each film underwent a stan­dardized schedule for the investigation of the effect of water vapor on N2 and CO2 permeabilities. Starting from dry conditions for both gases the procedure began with a first run of tests made with humid nitrogen gradually increasing the RH from 0 % to 80 %. Afterward, both gases were tested under dry conditions (0 % RH).

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: t run of tests made with humid nitrogen gradually increasing the RH from 0 % to 80 %. Afterward, both gases were tested under dry conditions (0 % RH). Then, a humidity ramp was applied for carbon dioxide, this procedure was followed by a final dry-state test for both gases. Each ramp took about 5 days to be completed. The cycle was then repeated, starting with a second nitrogen run at 0 % RH, and continued until constant permeability values were achieved for both CO2 and N2; the results of such an experimental campaign are reported in Figs. 7–9. In particular, the permeability of CO2 and N2 concerning relative hu­midity is shown in Fig. 7a and b for PIM-EA-TB, Fig. 8a and b for PIMSBF, and Fig. 9a and b for AO PIM-1 respectively. In Fig. 7b, humid CO2 permeability in PIM-EA-TB shows an evident

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 decreasing profile with the relative humidity increase; It changed indeed from 2285 Barrer in dry conditions to 250 Barrer at 80 %RH for the first run. The same trend is followed by N2 permeability which from 66 Barrer drops down to 15 Barrer at 80 %RH. Afterward, the following runs show a less pronounced decrease until a flattened profile where CO2 permeability stabilized around 500 Barrer in dry conditions and 250 Barrer at 80 %RH. Interestingly the value at 80 % RH of CO2 and N2 is immediately stabilized and does not change substantially between the first and the last permeation test for both nitrogen and carbon dioxide. PIM-SBF follows the same trend as shown in Fig. 8and b. Firstly, from a starting value of 152 Barrer for N2 in dry condition a value of 38 Barrer was reached at 80 % relative humidity. For CO2 on the other hand the initial dry state permeability value (about 4070 Barrer) dropped to 2000 Barrer after nitrogen testing and then down to 1150 Barrer when the relative humidity was brought to 80 %. After that, the profile with respect to RH flattened quite quickly and no major differences were observed in dry and high RH conditions after the third repetition for both nitrogen and CO2. The final permeabilities were in this case in the order of 550–700 Barrer for CO2 and 20–30 Barrer for N2 with lower values always obtained at 80 % RH.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: he final permeabilities were in this case in the order of 550–700 Barrer for CO2 and 20–30 Barrer for N2 with lower values always obtained at 80 % RH. Interestingly, for hydrophilic AO-PIM-1, a different situation is seen in Fig. 9and b. As with the other PIMs previously seen, AO-PIM-1 shows a clear permeability decrease (more than 1 order of magnitude) with increasing relative humidity for both nitrogen and carbon dioxide. However, when the water is removed the initial permeability is almost completely restored and remains well above the values observed at high humidity. As an example, dry CO2 permeability passes from 1954 Barrer to 1600 Barrer after the first contact with water and then to 1100 Barrer after the third repetition. The values observed at 80 % RH, on the other hand, are lower and steady around the values of 120 Barrer in the different tests performed. In line with what is already observed for water sorption therefore, the experimental data shows some common behaviors and some clear differences among the different materials. First, the common decrease of permeability at increasing relative humidity suggests that, despite the different polarity and hydrophilicity of the different PIMs, they remain rigid glassy polymers where swelling is not activated even at high hu­midity. Therefore, competitive sorption and pore-filling mechanisms

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 control the permeation in the presence of water. More in detail, at increasing relative humidity, water molecules progressively fill the available free volume within the polymers, thus lowering gas solubility in the matrix and at the same time hindering gas transport across the membrane. [4,15,39,50]. The other interesting result is related to the fact that such a decrease is not completely reversible and, in the case of PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF, leads to a reduced and almost RH-independent permeability after only three repetitions or 30 days of testing. To better understand this behavior, it is useful to compare present results with previous studies related to the studied PIMs, obtained in the absence of moisture or in the presence of other polar solvents such as methanol. Concerning the case of absence of moisture, it has been previously reported in the literature, that the trend of carbon dioxide reduction for PIM-EA-TB exhibits an initial sharp decrease of approximately 50 % within the first 2 days, followed by a more gradual reduction of 50 % after 470 days [26]. This suggests that, in the absence of moisture, this polymer experiences an initial permeability drop, likely due to early structural adjustments or plasticization effects, followed by a slower, long-term decline occurring as the polymer matrix undergoes ageing.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: e to early structural adjustments or plasticization effects, followed by a slower, long-term decline occurring as the polymer matrix undergoes ageing. This behavior is indeed rather different from what was observed in this study conducted under humid conditions. Here, indeed, the CO2 permeability of PIM-EA-TB decreases significantly, from 2285 Barrer to 1000 Barrer after just one week, to stabilize after 3 weeks, at around 500 Barrer. Also methanol, on the other hand, has been shown to cause accelerated ageing of the material, similar to the effect observed with water vapor in the current analysis; methanol permeability in PIM-EA-TB indeed started decreasing after only 2 h of analysis and reduced to less than half of the initial values after about 500 h of testing [49]. Although the interactions between these penetrants and the polymer matrix may differ from that of water (as an example, methanol shows solubility definitely higher than water in PIM-EA-TB [41]), both components are highly polar and may induce comparable effects on the membrane structure and transport properties. For PIM-SBF, the situation is similar; literature values under dry conditions report an initial permeability of 8850 Barrer, which drops to 2410 Barrer over 2088 days, corresponding to a decrease of nearly 70 % [47].

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: conditions report an initial permeability of 8850 Barrer, which drops to 2410 Barrer over 2088 days, corresponding to a decrease of nearly 70 % [47]. However, in the tests performed in the presence of water, a faster decline has been observed, with permeability decreasing by approxi­mately 50 % each week, followed by stabilization after two weeks. For these two polymers, therefore, there seems to be a clear effect of water on polymer “ageing” defined as permanent permeability decrease with time. Both PIM-EA-TB and PIM-SBF polymers show, after a few weeks of humid testing, permeability values in dry conditions well below those usually observed in several months of dry ageing tests. More than that, the final state results to be no more affected by exposure to moisture, suggesting the attainment of a more stable configuration likely denser than what could be obtained by dry ageing. For AO-PIM-1, to the best of our knowledge, no extensive data on polymer ageing are available, but, after the same type of testing, a clear difference remains between gas permeability in dry conditions and in highly humid environments. This behavior correlates with sorption data, where a lower reduction was observed in the case of AO-PIM-1 with respect to other polymers.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: nvironments. This behavior correlates with sorption data, where a lower reduction was observed in the case of AO-PIM-1 with respect to other polymers. It also suggests that while in the first two materials, the presence of water causes an irreversible reduction of free volume, in AO-PIM-1, such reduction is reversible, or almost reversible, even if no or limited swelling seems to be present, also in this case, at least considering what observed in permeation tests. Finally, it is worthwhile to notice that, at higher relative humidity, the same value of permeation is observed for a single material in the different repetitions. This suggests that, at least at the highest RH considered, the amount of free volume still available for gas transport is constant, or the maximum occupation of free volume by water mole­cules is not changed during subsequent testing. These findings indicate that the reduction in water sorption is primarily related to successive decreases in free volume, rather than to any modification in polymer- water interactions. Concerning the separation ability of the membranes, the influence of humidity on membrane selectivity is better represented through a Robeson’s plot.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: Concerning the separation ability of the membranes, the influence of humidity on membrane selectivity is better represented through a Robeson’s plot. Indeed, even if the present data were taken in humid conditions and cannot be directly compared with those used to draw the Upper Bound, this kind of qualitative comparison is a good indication of the ability or inability of the membrane to carry on the CO2/N2 sepa­ration when water vapor is present in the system. For this reason, the position of PIM-EA-TB, PIM-SBF and AO-PIM-1 results in relation to the CO2/N2 Upper Bound are reported respectively in Fig 10–12 where dry and humid data (at the maximum RH tested) are compared. The selectivity data are somewhat scattered, likely due to error propagation, which leads to uncertainty in the order of 10 %; however, general trends seem to be clear and rather different for the three ma­terials. Indeed, in the case of PIM-EA-TB, not only the permeability but also the selectivity decreases with increasing humidity. Reductions from 55 to about 20 are obtained by increasing RH with differences that become less and less dependent on RH in the subsequent repetitions and remain substantially constant in the third permeation run. On the other hand, the opposite behavior is observed for the other two polymers, which both show an increase in selectivity for humid tests. In this con­dition, indeed, N2 permeability decreases more than CO2 in the presence of water.

citation.title: Effect of water vapor on CO2 and N2 permeability in PIM based membranes

content: ch both show an increase in selectivity for humid tests. In this con­dition, indeed, N2 permeability decreases more than CO2 in the presence of water. The effect is rather limited in the case of PIM-SBF, for which selectivity shows a threefold increase from 10 to about 30, while it is more pronounced for AO-PIM-1, which reaches selectivity above 200 at high RH resulting in data above the 2019 Robeson upper bound for this important gas pair [17]. This phenomenon is typical in many polymers and can be related to ageing, which in PIMs is often accompanied by selectivity increases [51]. However, in this case the increase in selectivity observed in AO-PIM-1 cannot be explained by ageing alone, as it is obtained already in the first tests at high humidity and disappears when the polymer is tested again in dry conditions. Moreover, ageing in polymer membranes can enhance selectivity but seldom to the level seen in this case: ex­amples from literature show increase in selectivity due to ageing from 15.9 up to 21 in 100 days for PIM-Trip-TB CO2/N2 [24], or from 14.9 to 17.7 in 39 days, for PIM-TMN-Trip [26]. Thus, the high performance obtained for AO-PIM-1 may be attributed to a facilitated transport mechanism enabled by the amidoxime (AO) group, which is well known to interact selectively with CO2 in the presence of water [52]. 3.3. FT-IR spectra FTIR spectroscopy was conducted on all membranes before testing,

content: E. Medri et al. Journal of Membrane Science 731 (2025) 124233 that is after methanol treatment and drying, as well as after the last humid testing (both permeation and sorption) to determine whether the exposure to humidity caused any significant change in the different PIMs. The results, presented in Figure 13–15 for PIM -SBF, PIM-EA-TB and AO-PIM-1, respectively, revealed the characteristic functional groups associated with each base material. For PIM-EA-TB, peaks at 2960 cm -1 and 1420 cm-1 indicated the CH2 asymmetric stretch vi­brations and the scissoring vibrations of the ethanoanthracene (EA) unit [40]; PIM-SBF exhibited bands at 2238 cm-1 (C ––– N), 1607 cm-1 (ar­omatic C––C bending), 1470-1430 cm-1 (-C-H bending within –CH2and -C-CH3 groups), and 1300-1000 cm-1 (-C-Ostretching) [53]. Finally, AO-PIM-1 spectra show C––N stretching band at 1660 cm1, N–O stretching vibration at 914 cm-1 of oxime groups and the broad peaks at 3482 and 3306 cm-1 are attributed to the O–H and N–H bonds origi­nating from the amidoxime groups [54]. It is interesting to notice that in PIM-SBF tested samples, shown in Fig. 13, there is a new peak that appears at 1720 cm-1, a band usually related to the presence of carboxyl groups in the matrix. This same peak, has been observed in PIM-1 by Lasseuguette et al. [15] who related it to the acidic hydrolysis of the nitrile (-C ––– N) groups in the films that were subjected to humid CO2. Indeed, it was found to grow proportionally Fig. 11.
